items.rationale: After the FTX/Celsius/Terra blowups, the house frames transparent, self-custodial, overcollateralized on-chain finance as the structural answer to CeFi failure — 'can't be evil > don't be evil.'
items.age_note: bullish since 2021 DeFi-summer framing; conviction reaffirmed and sharpened post-FTX into a CeFi-vs-DeFi contrast

citations.quote: Do the collapses of FTX, Celsius, and Terra signal the end of crypto? No, but they do show why we need #DeFi.
DeFi is better policy than CeFi. Can't be evil > Don't be evil
decentralization, transparency, and trustlessness of blockchain tech can protect users better than any... regime in CeFi ever could

items.rationale: Repeatedly called the most underappreciated primitive — succinctness for scaling rollups plus encryption to unlock private DeFi, identity, gaming and broad adoption; backed Aztec, Aleo, zkSync.
items.age_note: consistent from 2021 (zkSync) through Dec 2022 (Aztec); conviction rising, not reversed

citations.quote: Encryption & privacy will be essential for blockchains to broaden their utility and gain widespread adoption.
Zero Knowledge Proofs will completely transform the web3 tech stack in the coming years.
i still think people underestimate how transformational zero knowledge proofs are

items.rationale: Token incentives bootstrapping real-world networks (wireless, storage, compute, energy) to undercut entrenched incumbents; actively reframing the category in Jan 2023 with a unit-economics test.
items.age_note: Helium thesis since 2021; freshly re-articulated as DePIN/Open Source Services in Jan 2023

citations.quote: The essential question when considering building a DePIN network: Verification cost < incumbent margin. big if true
Blockchains can provide any number of services, compute, storage, cell phone service, energy storage, physical transportation
Helium's use of crypto incentives spurred the world's fastest-growing wireless network

items.rationale: Newest frontier — crypto gives machines ownership/coordination, enabling autonomous applications and crowdsourced data/models/compute; an emerging late-2022/early-2023 theme.
items.age_note: seeded 2021 (Runloop, kosenlabs); elevated to a headline thesis Dec 2022–Jan 2023

citations.quote: AI = how machines can think; crypto = how machines can own; AI + crypto = how machines can think on their own
AI + crypto = collective AI. Crypto mechanisms can incentivize the crowdsourcing of better data sets, machine learning models, and compute.

items.rationale: Core thesis that tokens are programmable 'value vectors' (not mere shares or money), a generalizable primitive like the website that anchors web3's design space.
items.age_note: persisted and restated from 2021 through Jan 2023, framing evolving toward 'value vectors'

citations.quote: Tokens are value vectors. Dollars are value scalars. Tokens matter because they allow builders to keep the high dimensionality inherent in value representations
tokens are better thought of as a new digital primitive, analogous to the website

items.rationale: Genuine ownership, dramatically lower take rates vs web2, and community-driven value; maturing toward standardized licensing (Can't Be Evil) and a Pokémon-card-like collectibles model.
items.age_note: highly bullish 2021–2022; by late 2022 enthusiasm tempered toward sustainability/utility over speculation, but not reversed

citations.quote: we're thrilled to be launching a slate of open sourced 'Can't be Evil' NFT licenses
the long run digital collectibles business model will look more like Pokemon cards than the ultra-scarcity pervasive in NFTs today

items.rationale: Internet-native coordination that can be 10,000x cheaper than legal machinery; house has invested heavily in legal frameworks (UNA), governance research and tooling to make them viable.
items.age_note: strong 2021 enthusiasm; by mid-2022 expectations explicitly tempered ('city governments, not dynamic startups')

citations.quote: DAOs are fundamental to the success of web3, yet they're shrouded in legal uncertainty with no consensus about a path to legitimacy.
Progressive decentralization is possible... Decentralization needn't be 'all-or-nothing.' But you have to plan in advance

items.rationale: Ethereum viewed as the clear leader with deepest developer mindshare and economic security; the Merge a major upgrade, with rollups (optimistic + ZK) the scaling path despite acknowledged latency/prover limits.
items.age_note: consistent through 2022; Merge completed Sept 2022 and judged a success

citations.quote: Once it's done, I believe Ethereum will be a far superior blockchain than it was before.
other layer 1 blockchains have grown rapidly over the last year, but Ethereum is the clear leader

items.rationale: The single biggest, most-repeated concern: opaque SEC/CFTC enforcement without guidance is the top economic drag, drives builders offshore, and threatens to regulate protocols (ending decentralization) rather than apps.
items.age_note: persistent throughout corpus; intensity peaked late 2022–Jan 2023 after FTX and CFTC v. Ooki DAO

citations.quote: Regulation-by-enforcement has failed. Our approach has to change.
Disappointing to see the CFTC doubling down on regulation-by-enforcement with its overly broad and ambiguous complaint.
Every week a founder will ask me whether they should build outside the US to avoid regulatory uncertainty

items.rationale: FTX, Celsius, Voyager, BlockFi, 3AC all framed as preventable centralized-trust failures ('not decentralized'); custody is a liability and trust must be regulated differently than trustless software.
items.age_note: sharpened dramatically Nov 2022 after FTX; consistent with earlier 'not your keys' stance

citations.quote: Shoutout to the CeFi players out there demonstrating unequivocally why trust inherently needs to be regulated differently than trustless.
The questionable practices of many of these businesses were widely known... yet no preventative action was taken.

items.rationale: After Terra/UST, bad collateral (not algorithms per se) named the root cause of collapse; under-collateralized, endogenously-backed designs dismissed as meme coins, while collateralized decentralized stables are praised.
items.age_note: hardened after May 2022 UST collapse; nuance maintained that well-collateralized stables are a major positive

citations.quote: Uncollateralized stablecoins are basically just meme coins with no potential upside.
bad collateral is the root cause of recent collapses like Terra, not 'algorithmic stablecoins'

items.rationale: Fraud repeatedly called the biggest risk to web3 participants; NFT roadmaps/financialization likened to 2017-18 unregistered-securities hype; speculation distracts from durable value.
items.age_note: persistent; NFT-specific financialization concern voiced Feb 2022 and never retracted

citations.quote: Fraud continues to be the biggest risk to web3 participants
project roadmaps... are mostly transparent attempts create hype cycles that are reminiscent of the ICO/unregistered securities offerings of 2017/2018

items.rationale: Token-weighted direct democracy seen as failing — voter apathy lets small groups push proposals through; the house argues web3 needs indirect/representative democracy, delegation and oversight.
items.age_note: articulated mid-2022 governance research; a maturation concern, not a reversal of DAO enthusiasm

citations.quote: you simply can't govern through direct democracy at any kind of meaningful scale
pretty small groups of people who can benefit from getting certain things passed are going to start pushing to pass things

items.rationale: Even as a research booster, the house warns that prioritizing performance over security (e.g. ~80-bit deployments) and slow prover runtimes could erode trust and cap practical applications.
items.age_note: raised Aug–Sept 2022; technical caution paired with structural ZK optimism

citations.quote: if we prioritize performance over security, we may cause people to lose trust in the technology and potentially see misappropriated funds
the prover computation in these snarks is going to increasingly be a problem

items.rationale: Clear leader by developer mindshare, fees and economic security; post-Merge judged superior and more secure; explicitly argued not to be a security due to decentralization.
items.age_note: consistently bullish 2021–2022; Merge completed Sept 2022

citations.quote: Ethereum is the clear leader. Its overwhelming mindshare among web3 devs helps explains why users pay more than $15 million in average daily fees
I believe Ethereum will be a far superior blockchain than it was before.

items.rationale: Flagship example of token incentives bootstrapping a real-world network; cited as proof crypto applications go well beyond currencies.
items.age_note: championed heavily 2021–early 2022; most recent explicit Helium praise Mar 2022, not restated by Jan 2023

citations.quote: It's a perfect example of how crypto applications go well beyond crypto-currencies.
Helium today is the fastest growing wireless network ever. It has now blown past the 100,000 hotspot mark

items.rationale: Praised as underwriting protocol risk and surviving volatility; collateralized, exogenously-backed stablecoins held up as a capital-efficiency unlock and the right model vs Terra.
items.age_note: DAI defended vs Terra in Dec 2022; governance-as-risk-token framing mid-2022

citations.quote: Plenty of examples that have successfully navigated all the recent volatility, and those tokens are a huge unlock for capital efficiency
The difference between endogenous and exogenous collateral is pretty significant.

items.rationale: Collapsed due to under-collateralization and endogenous collateral; held up repeatedly as the cautionary archetype for what not to build.
items.age_note: collapsed May 2022; cited through Dec 2022 as the canonical bad-collateral failure

citations.quote: LUNA - Not decentralized
equating the risk profiles of Maker and Terra... Terra being extremely undercollateralized

items.rationale: Symbol of the centralized-exchange collapse; widely doubted in the legal community before it imploded, and grouped under 'not decentralized.'
items.age_note: collapsed Nov 2022; commentary contemporaneous

citations.quote: FTT was widely viewed with skepticism in the legal community
FTX/FTT - Not decentralized.

items.rationale: Endorsed the view that its meme provenance leaves it without a fundamental economic reason to thrive.
items.age_note: stated once Nov 2021; never restated — old but uncontradicted

citations.quote: its provenance as a meme leaves it without much of a fundamental economic reason to thrive. Fair.

risk_regime.rationale: Despite the 2022 crypto winter and FTX fallout, the house is structurally risk-on: it raised a new fund declaring the 'golden era of web3,' insists 'we're still so early' (~internet in 1995), and frames the underlying price-innovation cycle as continuing to advance even through downturns. The risk-on conviction is long-horizon and builder-focused, not a near-term price call — tactically it is sober and defensive, emphasizing decentralization, sound collateral, DeFi-over-CeFi, and regulatory survival. The dominant near-term posture is caution toward centralized intermediaries and regulatory overhang, layered on top of high structural conviction.

notes: Corpus is a16z crypto team voices (cdixon, Chris Lehane/policy lead 'NYcryptolawyer', Eddy Lazzarin, Tim Roughgarden, Scott Kominers, Ali Yahya/Arianna Simpson investment voices, plus academic transcripts). Recency weighting matters most for: (1) the CeFi/DeFi split, which crystallized only after Nov 2022 FTX collapse; (2) stablecoins, where the post-Terra (May 2022+) view distinguishes good vs bad collateral rather than condemning 'algorithmic' designs; (3) DAOs and NFTs, where 2021 exuberance was explicitly tempered toward sustainability, governance realism and standardized licensing by late 2022 (recorded in age_notes, not treated as full reversals). The loudest and most recent single theme is regulatory: 'regulate apps, not protocols,' decentralization as the dividing line for securities law, and condemnation of regulation-by-enforcement — this is more a policy-risk posture than a market-sector view but dominates the Jan-2023 corpus. Emerging late-corpus excitement (AI×crypto, DePIN/Open Source Services, ZK/encryption) is rising in conviction and weighted up accordingly. No statement in the corpus is dated after 2023-01-31; nothing about future events was used.
